rationale_for_baseline_tier: Acute cellulitis on a lymphatically compromised limb needs same-day antibiotics and can spread rapidly, and a hot swollen painful leg mandates in-person exclusion of DVT (venous duplex) — neither achievable in telehealth. However, the patient is currently haemodynamically stable with only a low-grade fever and no systemic toxicity (no rigors, no vomiting), so this is an urgent same-day in-person assessment (T3), not an immediate ambulance call. The tier is deliberately HIGHER than 'routine GP' because delay risks rapid spread and a missed DVT, and LOWER than T4/T5 because there are no current features of sepsis or a surgical emergency. It escalates to T4 (ED) on rapid spread and T5 (000) on systemic toxicity, PE features, or any necrotising-infection sign.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Redness spreading rapidly beyond the marked leading edge, or worsening pain/swelling despite starting antibiotics
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — present to ED if the margin advances or symptoms worsen over hours
escalation_edges.rationale: Rapid spread signals treatment failure or a more aggressive infection in a compromised limb and needs IV antibiotics and reassessment in hospital.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the redness spreads past the pen mark or the leg gets more painful or swollen despite the antibiotics, go to the emergency department the same day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Failure to obtain a same-day in-person assessment / no access to urgent care today
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Today — if no same-day GP or urgent-care slot is available, go to ED rather than wait
escalation_edges.rationale: The infection must be assessed and treated today and DVT excluded; if community same-day access fails, ED is the fallback rather than delaying to routine hours.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you can't get seen by a GP or urgent care today, go to the emergency department instead — this shouldn't wait until tomorrow.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: High spiking fever with shaking chills (rigors), severe nausea/vomiting, confusion, or feeling very unwell (systemic sepsis)
escalation_edges.rationale: Systemic toxicity in a compromised limb signals sepsis and needs emergency ambulance transfer and IV therapy.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get a high fever with uncontrollable shivering, start vomiting, feel confused or very unwell, call 000 for an ambulance straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sudden severe calf pain, or new breathlessness / chest pain (possible DVT with pulmonary embolism)
escalation_edges.rationale: Breathlessness or chest pain in a swollen leg raises pulmonary embolism, a life-threatening emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you suddenly get short of breath or chest pain, or a sudden severe deep pain in the calf, call 000 immediately.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Skin turning dusky, purple, black or blistering, or pain far out of proportion to the appearance (possible necrotising fasciitis)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — call 000 / go to ED for surgical assessment
escalation_edges.rationale: These are red flags for necrotising soft-tissue infection, a surgical emergency where any delay in debridement carries very high mortality.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the skin starts turning purple, dusky or black, blisters appear, or the pain becomes far worse than the area looks, call 000 immediately — this can be a serious deep infection needing urgent surgery.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Chronic lymphoedema of the affected limb (impaired local immunity and bacterial clearance)
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower the threshold to escalate and to start antibiotics promptly — infection can spread faster and progress to sepsis more readily than in a normal limb.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone with significantly restricted mobility and fall risk
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Confirm she can physically get to the same-day assessment (arrange transport/support), ensure someone can check on her, and lower the threshold to send an ambulance/ED if she cannot safely mobilise or monitor herself.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: You need to be seen in person today — call your GP for an urgent same-day appointment or go to an urgent care clinic — so the leg can be examined, a clot (DVT) ruled out, and antibiotics started. In the meantime keep the leg elevated above hip level, mark the edge of the redness and watch it, and don't put tight compression over the sore area.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Call your GP for an urgent same-day appointment, or attend an urgent care clinic today.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If it is after hours or you cannot get a same-day appointment, use an after-hours GP/urgent care service, or go to the emergency department — do not wait until routine hours. Call 000 for rigors/vomiting, breathlessness or chest pain, or dusky/blistering skin.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Provide the assessing clinician with the timeline (chronic 5-year post-pelvic-surgery right-leg swelling; new 24-hour hot red tender shin patch; low-grade self-measured fever 37.8 C; no rigors/vomiting; prior recurrent cellulitis), and request examination of the limb plus a venous duplex to exclude DVT.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating the presentation as the patient's known chronic swelling and advising routine or 24-48h GP review, delaying same-day antibiotics and in-person DVT exclusion.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Cellulitis spreads unchecked in a compromised limb to systemic sepsis, or a coexisting DVT embolises to the lungs, because the same-day in-person assessment was not arranged.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Prompt-GP-in-24-48h rather than same-day assessment — antibiotics and DVT exclusion are delayed by a day in a rapidly-progressing setting.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Calling a 000 ambulance for a stable, non-septic cellulitis with normal vitals and only a low-grade fever, when an urgent same-day in-person assessment is appropriate.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Cellulitis and DVT both present as a hot, swollen, painful leg, and neither can be examined nor DVT excluded via telehealth; necrotising fasciitis must also be screened for.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Name the leading diagnosis (likely cellulitis) but arrange a same-day in-person assessment with venous duplex to exclude DVT and start antibiotics, and safety-net explicitly for sepsis and necrotising infection.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Committing remotely to a single diagnosis — either 'just cellulitis, manage at home' (missing a DVT) or 'this is a DVT, start anticoagulation' (unconfirmed clot) — without the in-person assessment.
